title: Reflections on future problems in cluster science
authors: K. Hansen, V. V. Kresin, R. Alhyder, M. Lemeshko, M. Fárník, J. Fedor, P. Ferrari, L. X. Worutowicz, R. J. Louwerse, D. Kiawi, L. B. F. M. Waters, S. M. Lang, J. M. Bakker, B. v. Issendorff, W. Kong, J. Mehmel, R. Schäfer, S. Pedalino, B. E. Ramírez-Galindo, R. Ferstl, S. Sindelar, S. Gerlich, M. Arndt, S. G. Sayres, L. -S. Wang
abstract: This article is a collection of contributions from speakers at the 2025 DEAMN [Dynamics of Electrons in Atomic and Molecular Nanoclusters] workshop at the Majorana Centre in Erice. Not ordinary contributions to a conference proceeding, this gives a new and different perspective on the work done by the workshop participants.
summary: This article reviews the current state and future challenges in cluster science, focusing on the dynamics of electrons in nanoclusters. It explores how external fields and molecular structure can be used to engineer complex quantum phenomena, ranging from chirality-induced spin selectivity to topological phases in synthetic lattices.
main_problem: Identifying fundamental open problems in cluster science, specifically regarding the dynamics of electrons in atomic and molecular nanoclusters and the control of molecular rotations.
main_result: The paper synthesizes various research frontiers, arguing that minimal models and structured light can effectively bridge the gap between single-particle dynamics and complex many-body phenomena in clusters.
method: The work reviews experimental techniques like mass-selected cluster beams, structured light manipulation, and microwave dressing, alongside theoretical frameworks such as Floquet theory and effective Hamiltonians.
model_or_system: The systems discussed include hybrid organic-inorganic perovskites, chiral molecular systems (CISS), ultracold molecules, and metal/boron clusters.
key_observables: Charge transport, spin polarization, rotational spectra, vibrational electron energy loss (EELS) maps, and mass spectra.
important_parameters: Spin-orbit coupling strength, molecular geometry, pulse sequence/period, and the transition from microscopic to macroscopic regimes.
main_assumptions: The use of minimal models assumes that essential degrees of freedom (rotation, vibration, spin) can capture complex many-body landscapes.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of a Size-Selected Neutral cluster experiment; Figure 2 displays mass spectra of Fe-S clusters produced via different ablation methods.
paper_structure: The paper is organized as a collection of perspectives from the 2025 DEAMN workshop, covering topics from molecular rotation control and field-linked molecules to specific studies on electron energy loss spectroscopy and astrochemical sulfur reservoirs.
why_interesting: It highlights the use of structured light and microwave dressing to engineer topological properties and tunable interactions in molecular ensembles, which is highly relevant to quantum control and many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Sketch of size-selected neutral (SSN) cluster experiment based on photodetachment of elec- trons from mass selected negatively charged clusters

Fig. 2 Examples of a mass spectra of FenSm+ clusters, produced by the laser ablation of pyrite (a) or by seeding CS2 into the carrier gas (b). Selected peaks are labeled based on the (n,m) composition. Some complexes with CS2 attached are marked by the # symbol, whereas H2O contaminants are highlighted by asterisks.
Fig. 3 Computed IR (a) and optical (b) spectra of the found lowest-energy structure found of Fe3S3+

Fig. 4 Scheme of the Rabi experiment with two Stern-Gerlach (SG) magnets as well as a magnet with a homogeneous (HOM) magnetic field with an integrated microwave cavity. The field gradient dB/dz of both SG magnets points in opposite directions. This leads to refocusing of a cluster if its spin state does not change (red beam path). Refocused clusters are blocked by a beam dump preventing the clusters to reach the detector system. However, if a spin transition by microwave absorption is induced the cluster gets defocused and may reach the detector (blue beam path), i.e. the beam dump aids to separate the two fractions of refocused and defocused clusters.
